Metadata.Case_Name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

Party_Details.Name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd was incorporated on 9 February 2009.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd was in the business of marketing and selling Australian real estate on behalf of Australian property developers.

Australian Property Group Pte Ltd earned revenue from commissions paid by Australian property developers, consisting of a Front End Commission upon signing of an unconditional sale contract and a Back End Commission upon completion.
On incorporation, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd had directors and shareholders including Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian, Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd received a loan of $900,000 from Goh Cheng Liang pursuant to a loan agreement dated 11 March 2009.

Non-executive directors Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren acted to protect the interests of lender Goh Cheng Liang while the executive directors controlled the day-to-day operations of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd.
On 15 May 2009, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene joined Australian Property Group Pte Ltd as its general manager.

After May 2009, Sean Colville Niven managed the finances of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd and secured marketing rights from Australian property developers, while Wang Zijian was sales manager and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene oversaw overall operations and the sales team.
In November 2009, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd borrowed money from Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd and used it to repay the $900,000 loan from Goh Cheng Liang.
On 7 November 2009, the non-executive directors of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd resigned and their shares were subsequently transferred to Sean Colville Niven.

On 8 December 2009, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene was appointed a director of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd and in January 2010 she became a shareholder by purchasing 240,000 shares from Sean Colville Niven for $160,000.
On 21 March 2011, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd was placed under judicial management and Timothy James Reid of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd was appointed as judicial manager.
After his appointment, the judicial manager of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd discovered that certain directors had not paid for their shares although the shares had been issued as fully paid up.

After his appointment, the judicial manager of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd identified numerous payments made to director Sean Colville Niven which he considered unjustifiable due to lack of documentary support.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd engaged Firstwaters Consultants Pte Ltd to reconstruct its accounts from 2009 and to maintain its day-to-day accounting.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd engaged chartered accountant John Kennedy Stuart to provide tax services and advice to its clients and later to investigate and report on its financial situation.
On 2 July 2010, chartered accountant John Kennedy Stuart advised the directors of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd that the company was technically insolvent based on its cash flow and commitments.

On 6 September 2010, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd entered into an agreement with its directors and shareholder Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene under which she agreed to capitalise $175,000 owed to her and inject $150,000 in exchange for 325,000 new shares.

Under the 6 September 2010 agreement, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd agreed to write off 40% of a debt of $300,450.37 owed by Sean Colville Niven and to accept repayment of the balance in instalments.

Under the 6 September 2010 agreement, the shareholders of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd agreed on their respective liabilities for unpaid share capital and to repay 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
On 6 October 2010, the directors of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd resolved to appoint Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to conduct a review of the company’s accounts.

On 11 October 2010, Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd issued a draft summary of findings indicating that many payments by Australian Property Group Pte Ltd, including payments to directors, lacked supporting vouchers and documentation.

On 13 October 2010, Australian Property Group Pte Ltd and its three directors signed a handwritten document referred to as the October Agreement dealing with resignation of directors, transfer of shares and settlement of liabilities.
On 1 March 2011, director Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene applied to place Australian Property Group Pte Ltd under judicial management.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd commenced this action in July 2011 through its judicial manager to recover moneys allegedly wrongfully paid to its directors.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Australian Property Group Pte Ltd could recover unpaid share capital and various payments made to or for the benefit of its directors, and whether prior agreements and alleged authorisations barred the company from doing so.
Party_Details.Rule: A company may seek recovery of unpaid share capital and unauthorised payments made to directors; directors owe fiduciary and statutory duties to act in the best interests of the company and, when the company is insolvent or near insolvency, to have regard to the interests of creditors; appropriation of company funds for a director's personal use is prima facie an abuse of funds and a breach of duty unless properly authorised and in the company's interests; transactions between an insolvent company and its directors that diminish assets available to creditors may be voidable at the instance of the company.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the business model and financial position of Australian Property Group Pte Ltd, including its reliance on Front End and Back End Commissions, its loans from Goh Cheng Liang and Ubertas, and its cash flow difficulties. The court considered the roles of the executive and non-executive directors, the reconstruction of the company’s accounts, and the findings of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd regarding unsupported payments. The court analysed the September and October Agreements to determine whether they involved write-offs or rearrangements of debts and unpaid share capital owed to the company by its directors at a time when the company was technically insolvent or in serious financial difficulty. The court assessed whether alleged oral arrangements and internal practices could amount to proper authorisation for the use of the company’s funds for directors’ personal expenses, and whether such arrangements were consistent with the company’s constitutional documents and the interests of its creditors.
Party_Details.Conclusion: Judgment entered for Australian Property Group Pte Ltd against the second and third defendants for unpaid share capital, repayment of specified sums paid for the second defendant’s personal use, and an account and repayment of sums paid into the defendants’ joint account, with interest and costs.

Metadata.Case_Name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

Party_Details.Name: H.A. & Chung Partnership

Facts.Text: H.A. & Chung Partnership was the first defendant in the action and was described as a law firm.
The first defendant settled the claim brought against it by Australian Property Group Pte Ltd before the conclusion of the proceedings against the second and third defendants.
Australian Property Group Pte Ltd had paid certain legal fees to the first defendant in relation to Suit No 170 of 2009 involving JL Property Group Pte Ltd, Mr Niven, Mr Wang and others.
Some of the legal fees that Australian Property Group Pte Ltd sought to recover from Mr Niven had already been recovered from the first defendant.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether H.A. & Chung Partnership, as the first defendant law firm, was liable to Australian Property Group Pte Ltd in respect of payments it had received, and how those payments affected the sums recoverable from the remaining defendants.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company has made payments to a third party law firm on behalf of its directors, any recovery of those sums from the law firm reduces the amount that can be claimed from the directors for the same payments.
Party_Details.Application: The court considered the legal fees paid by Australian Property Group Pte Ltd to the first defendant in connection with litigation involving Mr Niven and examined how amounts already recovered from the first defendant should be deducted from the sums claimed against Mr Niven.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The claim against H.A. & Chung Partnership was settled and the proceedings continued only against the second and third defendants.
